title: Afraegle gabonensis
intro: Afraegle gabonensis, also known as African mahogany or Gabonese kegelle, is a medicinal plant species belonging to the Meliaceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it useful in the treatment of various diseases such as fever, pain, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Afraegle gabonensis include limonoids, triterpenoids, and flavonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Afraegle gabonensis is commonly prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is also used in the production of traditional herbal remedies such as teas, tonics, and ointments.
taxonomy: Afraegle gabonensis is classified within the family Rutaceae, which includes citrus and related plants. This species belongs to the genus Afraegle, which comprises a small group of African plants. Afraegle gabonensis is further classified as a species within the Rutaceae family. It is native to the tropical regions of West and Central Africa. Its classification as a member of the Rutaceae family reflects its shared characteristics with other plants in this group.
uses: Afraegle gabonensis has been traditionally used in medicine in various parts of Africa. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antifungal properties, which can be beneficial in treating various health conditions. The roots, leaves, and bark of Afraegle gabonensis are used in traditional medicine to treat fever, skin infections, and digestive issues.
